subject: Mathematics
q: The area (in sq. units) of the smaller of the two circles that touch the parabola, <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math> at the point <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and the <math><mi>x</mi></math> -axis is
question: The area (in sq. units) of the smaller of the two circles that touch the parabola, <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math> at the point <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and the <math><mi>x</mi></math> -axis is
solution:
The equation of the tangent to the parabola y2=4ax at x1, y1 is yy1=2ax+x1. 

Equation of tangent to the parabola y2=4x at 1, 2 is
y⋅2=2x+1

⇒x-y+1=0   ...i

The equation of the family of circles touching a line l=0 at x1, y1 is x-x12+y-y12+λl=0.

Equation of family of circle touching the parabola at 1, 2 is

x-12+y-22+λx-y+1=0   ...ii

Since circle touches x-axis, hence by putting y=0, we get

x2+λ-2x+λ+5=0

As the circle touches the x-axis, hence this equation will have only one root.

And we know that a quadratic equationax2+bx+x=0, a≠0,  has only one root when its discriminant D=b2-4ac is zero.

Now D=0

⇒λ-22-4⋅1⋅λ+5=0

⇒λ2-4λ+4-4λ-20=0

⇒λ2-8λ-16=0

Now, applying the Sridharacharya formula, we get

λ=--8±-82-4×1×-162×1

⇒λ=4±42 for smaller circle λ=4-42.

Hence, the equation of the circle is x-12+y-22+4-42x-y+1=0

⇒x2+y2-2x1-22-2y4-22+9-42=0

Since, the circle touches the x-axis, hence the absolute value of the y-co-ordinate of the circle is the radius of the circle.

Thus, the radius of smaller circle r=4-22 units.

Area of circle πr2=π4-222

=π16-162+8=π24-162

=8π3-22 sq units.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let one end of a focal chord of the parabola $y^{2}=16 x$ be $(16,16)$. If $\mathrm{P}(\alpha, \beta)$ divides this focal chord internally in the ratio $5: 2$, then the minimum value of $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to :
question: Let one end of a focal chord of the parabola $y^{2}=16 x$ be $(16,16)$. If $\mathrm{P}(\alpha, \beta)$ divides this focal chord internally in the ratio $5: 2$, then the minimum value of $\alpha+\beta$ is equal to :
solution:
Parabola $y^2 = 16x$, $a = 4$, focus at $(4, 0)$. Point $(16, 16)$ has parameter $t_1 = 2$.
Other end of focal chord: $t_2 = -1/t_1 = -1/2$, giving $(4 \cdot 1/4, 8 \cdot (-1/2)) = (1, -4)$.
P divides the chord in ratio 5:2 internally (two cases):
From $(16,16)$ to $(1,-4)$: $P = \left(\frac{37}{7}, \frac{12}{7}\right)$, $\alpha + \beta = 7$.
From $(1,-4)$ to $(16,16)$: $P = \left(\frac{82}{7}, \frac{72}{7}\right)$, $\alpha + \beta = 22$.
Minimum value of $\alpha + \beta = 7$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $y^2=12 x$ be the parabola and $S$ be its focus. Let PQ be a focal chord of the parabola such that $(\mathrm{SP})(\mathrm{SQ})=\frac{147}{4}$. Let C be the circle described taking PQ as a diameter. If the equation of a circle $C$ is $64 x^2+64 y^2-\alpha x-64 \sqrt{3} y=\beta$, then $\beta-\alpha$ is equal to ________.
question: Let $y^2=12 x$ be the parabola and $S$ be its focus. Let PQ be a focal chord of the parabola such that $(\mathrm{SP})(\mathrm{SQ})=\frac{147}{4}$. Let C be the circle described taking PQ as a diameter. If the equation of a circle $C$ is $64 x^2+64 y^2-\alpha x-64 \sqrt{3} y=\beta$, then $\beta-\alpha$ is equal to ________.
solution:
$\mathrm{y}^2=12 \mathrm{x} \quad \mathrm{a}=3 \quad \mathrm{SP} \times \mathrm{SQ}=\frac{147}{4}$

Let $\mathrm{P}\left(3 \mathrm{t}^2, 6 \mathrm{t}\right)$ and $\mathrm{t}_1 \mathrm{t}_2=-1$
(ends of focal chord)
So, $Q\left(\frac{3}{t^2}, \frac{-6}{t}\right)$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{S}(3,0) \\
& \mathrm{SP} \times \mathrm{SQ}=\mathrm{PM}_1 \times \mathrm{QM}_2
\end{aligned}$
(dist. from directrix)
$\begin{aligned}
& =\left(3+3 \mathrm{t}^2\right)\left(3+\frac{3}{\mathrm{t}^2}\right)=\frac{147}{4} \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{\left(1+\mathrm{t}^2\right)^2}{\mathrm{t}^2}=\frac{49}{12} \\
& \mathrm{t}^2=\frac{3}{4}, \frac{4}{3} \\
& \mathrm{t}= \pm \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}, \pm \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}}
\end{aligned}$
considering $\mathrm{t}=\frac{-\sqrt{3}}{2}$
$\mathrm{P}\left(\frac{9}{4},-3 \sqrt{3}\right) \text { and } \mathrm{Q}(4,4 \sqrt{3})$

Hence, diametric circle:
$\begin{aligned}
& (x-4)\left(x-\frac{9}{4}\right)+(y+3 \sqrt{3})(y-4 \sqrt{3})=0 \\
& \Rightarrow x^2+y^2-\frac{25}{4} x-\sqrt{3} y-27=0 \\
& \Rightarrow \alpha=400, \beta=1728 \\
& \beta-\alpha=1328
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math><mi>A</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>9</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> be points on the parabola, <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Let <math><mi>C</mi></math> be chosen on the arc <math><mi>A</mi><mi>O</mi><mi>B</mi></math> of the parabola, where <math><mi>O</mi></math> is the origin, such that the area of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>B</mi></math> is maximum. Then, the area (in sq. units) of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>B</mi></math> , is:
question: Let <math><mi>A</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>4</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>9</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> be points on the parabola, <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>.</mo></math> Let <math><mi>C</mi></math> be chosen on the arc <math><mi>A</mi><mi>O</mi><mi>B</mi></math> of the parabola, where <math><mi>O</mi></math> is the origin, such that the area of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>B</mi></math> is maximum. Then, the area (in sq. units) of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">Δ</mi><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi><mi>B</mi></math> , is:
solution:
Let the coordinates of C be t2, 2t.

Area of ∆ABC=12 t22t19614-41



∆=56+t-t2, t∈-2, 3

d∆dt=1-2t=0⇒t=12

d2△dt2=-2, hence area is maximum at t=12

∴    Δmax=1254=3114

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the focal chord $P Q$ of the parabola $y^2=4 x$ make an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the positive $x$-axis, where P lies in the first quadrant. If the circle, whose one diameter is PS, S being the focus of the parabola, touches the $y$-axis at the point $(0, \alpha)$, then $5 \alpha^2$ is equal to :
question: Let the focal chord $P Q$ of the parabola $y^2=4 x$ make an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the positive $x$-axis, where P lies in the first quadrant. If the circle, whose one diameter is PS, S being the focus of the parabola, touches the $y$-axis at the point $(0, \alpha)$, then $5 \alpha^2$ is equal to :
solution:
$\tan 60^{\circ}=\frac{2 \mathrm{t}-0}{\mathrm{t}^2-1}=\sqrt{3} \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}=\sqrt{3}$
$\therefore \mathrm{P}(3,2 \sqrt{3})$
Circle :
$(x-1)(x-3)+(y-0)(y-2 \sqrt{3})=0$
at $x=0$
$\Rightarrow 3+y^2-2 \sqrt{3} y=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{y}=\sqrt{3}=\alpha$
$5 \alpha^2=15$

subject: Mathematics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> are two distinct points on the parabola, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math>, with parameters <math><mi>t</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math>, respectively. If the normal at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> passes through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, then the minimum value of <math><msubsup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msubsup></math> , is
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> are two distinct points on the parabola, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math>, with parameters <math><mi>t</mi></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math>, respectively. If the normal at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> passes through <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, then the minimum value of <math><msubsup><mrow><mi>t</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msubsup></math> , is
solution:
Given Pt2, 2t and Qt12, 2t1, are parametric points on parabola
y2=4x
Slope of normal at P=-t

Since normal passes through Q, 

⇒  slope of normal at P = slope of PQ

⇒ -t=2t1-tt12-t2

⇒ -t=2t1+t

⇒t1=-t-2t

⇒t12=t2+4t2+4   ...1

t2+4t2≥2t2.4t2≥4,

Using AM≥GM

∴  t12≥4+4   from 1

⇒ t12≥8

∴   Minimum value=8

subject: Mathematics
q: Consider the circle $C: x^2+y^2=4$ and the parabola $P: y^2=8 x$. If the set of all values of $\alpha$, for which three chords of the circle $C$ on three distinct lines passing through the point $(\alpha, 0)$ are bisected by the parabola $P$ is the interval $(p, q)$, then $(2 q-p)^2$ is equal to ________
question: Consider the circle $C: x^2+y^2=4$ and the parabola $P: y^2=8 x$. If the set of all values of $\alpha$, for which three chords of the circle $C$ on three distinct lines passing through the point $(\alpha, 0)$ are bisected by the parabola $P$ is the interval $(p, q)$, then $(2 q-p)^2$ is equal to ________
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & T=S_1 \\ & x_1+y_1=x_1^2+y_1^2 \\ & \alpha x_1=x_1^2+y_1^2 \\ & \alpha\left(2 t^2\right)=4 t^4+16 t^2 \\ & \alpha=2 t^2+8 \\ & \frac{\alpha-8}{2}=t^2 \end{aligned}$
Also, $4 \mathrm{t}^4+16 \mathrm{t}^2-4 < 0$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{t}^2=-2+\sqrt{5} \\ & \alpha=4+2 \sqrt{5} \\ & \therefore \alpha \in(8,4+2 \sqrt{5}) \\ & \therefore(2 \mathrm{q}-\mathrm{p})^2=80 \end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: A triangle is formed by the tangents at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo></math> on the curves <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math>, and the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is the radius of its circumcircle, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to
question: A triangle is formed by the tangents at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>)</mo></math> on the curves <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math>, and the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> is the radius of its circumcircle, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>r</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to
solution:
Let

S1:y2=2x

S2:x2+y2=4x

P(2,2) is common point on S1& S2

Let T1 is tangent to S1 at P2,2, then

T1:2y=x+2

⇒T1:x-2y+2=0

And, T2 is tangent to S2 at P2,2, then

T2:2x+2y=2x+2

⇒T2:y=2

And,  L3:x+y+2=0

PQ=a=20

QR=b=8

RP=c=6

Also, ar∆PQR=12221-201-421

⇒ar∆PQR=12221-4-20-600

⇒ar∆PQR=12-60+2=6

∴r=abc4Δ=1604=10

⇒r2=10

subject: Mathematics
q: Two tangent lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are drawn from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></math>. If the lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are also tangent to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>r</mi></math>, then $17 r^2$ is equal to
question: Two tangent lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are drawn from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> to the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi>x</mi></math>. If the lines <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>l</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> are also tangent to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mi>r</mi></math>, then $17 r^2$ is equal to
solution:
Given, from point 2,0 tangents are drawn on 2y2=-x, these tangents also touches the circle x-52+y2=r2, then value of 17r2 is:

Now equation of tangents when point is outside will be y=mx-18m, now 2,0 will lie here so m=±14, putting the value of m we get,

x-4y=2 and 4y+x=2

Using distance of centre from tangent equal to radius, we get

5-0-217=r or 5+0-217=r

∴  r2=917

⇒ 17r2=9

subject: Mathematics
q: If the length of the latus rectum of a parabola, whose focus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and the tangent at its vertex is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi></math>, is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mi>a</mi></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: If the length of the latus rectum of a parabola, whose focus is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>a</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and the tangent at its vertex is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>a</mi></math>, is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="|" close="|"><mi>a</mi></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given the equation of tangent at vertex is x+y=a

and the focus is a,a

Now the perpendicular distance from focus to tangent is 

a+a-a2=a2

Also given length of latus rectum=16

i.e. a2=164=4

⇒a=42

subject: Mathematics
q: A circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> unit passes through the vertex and the focus of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and touches the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>α</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to ______.
question: A circle of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> unit passes through the vertex and the focus of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and touches the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>α</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to ______.
solution:
The vertex and focus of parabola y2=2x are V0,0 and S12,0 respectively

Let the equation of circle passing through vertex and focus of the parabola be x-h2+y-k2=4

∵ Circle passes through 0,0

⇒h2+k2=4     ⋯1

∵ Circle passes through 12,0

12-h2+k2=4

⇒h2+k2=h+154     ⋯2

On solving 1 and 2

4=h+154

⇒h=14

⇒k=634

here k=-634 is rejected as circle with centre 14,-634 can't touch the parabola y2=2x.

So the equation of circle will be x-142+k-6342=4

Since this circle touches the parabola y=x-142+α

i.e. α=2+634=8+634

4α-8=63

4α-82=63

subject: Mathematics
q: A line passing through the point $\mathrm{A}(-2,0)$, touches the parabola $P: y^2=x-2$ at the point $B$ in the first quadrant. The area, of the region bounded by the line AB , parabola P and the x -axis, is :-
question: A line passing through the point $\mathrm{A}(-2,0)$, touches the parabola $P: y^2=x-2$ at the point $B$ in the first quadrant. The area, of the region bounded by the line AB , parabola P and the x -axis, is :-
solution:
Tangent
$\begin{aligned}
& y=m(x+2) \\ & y^2=x-2 \\ & (m(n+2))^2=n-2 \\ & m^2 x^2+\left(4 m^2-1\right) x+\left(4 m^2+2\right)=0 \\ & D=0 \\ & \left(4 m^2-1\right)^2-4 m^2\left(4 m^2+2\right)=0 \\ & m=\frac{1}{4} \\ & y=\frac{1}{4}(n+2)
\end{aligned}$
and point of tangency $(6,2)$

$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Area } A=\int_0^2\left(\left(y^2+2\right)-(4 y-2)\right) d y \\ & A=\frac{8}{3}
\end{aligned}$
option (3)

subject: Mathematics
q:
Statement-1: The line $x-2 y=2$ meets the parabola, $y^2+2 x=0$ only at the point $(-2,-2)$.
Statement-2: The line $y=m x-\frac{1}{2 m}(m \neq 0)$ is tangent to the parabola, $y^2=-2 x$ at the point $\left(-\frac{1}{2 m^2},-\frac{1}{m}\right)$
question:
Statement-1: The line $x-2 y=2$ meets the parabola, $y^2+2 x=0$ only at the point $(-2,-2)$.
Statement-2: The line $y=m x-\frac{1}{2 m}(m \neq 0)$ is tangent to the parabola, $y^2=-2 x$ at the point $\left(-\frac{1}{2 m^2},-\frac{1}{m}\right)$
options: Statement-1 is true; Statement- 2 is false.
Statement-1 is true; Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is a correct explanation for statement-1.
Statement-1 is false; Statement-2 is true.
Statement-1 a true; Statement-2 is true; Statement-2 is not a correct explanation for statement-1.
solution:
Both statements are true and statement-2 is the correct explanation of statement-1
$\therefore \quad$ The straight line $y=m x+\frac{a}{m}$ is always a tangent to the parabola $y^2=4 a x$ for any value of $m$.
The co-ordinates of point of contact
$$
\left(\frac{a}{m^2}, \frac{2 a}{m}\right)
$$

q: A line is a common tangent to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn></math> and the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math>. question: A line is a common tangent to the circle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn></math> and the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math>. If the two points of contact <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>c</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>d</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> are distinct and lie in the first quadrant, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to ___ .
solution:
Let coordinate of point At2,2t  ∵a=1

equation of tangent at point A

yt=x+t2

x-ty+t2=0

centre of circle 3,0

Now Perpendicular distance = radius

3-0+t21+t2=3

3+t22=91+t2

9+t4+6t2=9+9t2

t=0,-3,3

So point A3,23

⇒a=3, b=23

(Since it lies in first quadrant)

For point B which is foot of perpendicular from centre 3,0 to the tangent x-3y+3=0

c-31=d-0-3=-3-0+34

subject: Mathematics
q: Let A and B be the two points of intersection of the line $y+5=0$ and the mirror image of the parabola $y^2=4 x$ with respect to the line $x+y+4=0$. If d denotes the distance between A and B , and a denotes the area of $\triangle S A B$, where $S$ is the focus of the parabola $y^2=4 x$, then the value of $(a+d)$ is $\qquad$ -
question: Let A and B be the two points of intersection of the line $y+5=0$ and the mirror image of the parabola $y^2=4 x$ with respect to the line $x+y+4=0$. If d denotes the distance between A and B , and a denotes the area of $\triangle S A B$, where $S$ is the focus of the parabola $y^2=4 x$, then the value of $(a+d)$ is $\qquad$ -
solution: To find image of $P\left(t^2, 2 t\right)$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{x-t^2}{1}=\frac{y-2 t}{1}=\frac{-2\left(t^2+2 t+4\right)}{1^2+1^2}=-(t+1)^2-3 \\ & x=t^2-(t+1)^2-3=-2 t-4 \\ & y=2 t-(t+1)^2-3=-t^2-4 \\ & t=\frac{-x-4}{2} \\ & \Rightarrow y+4=-\left(\frac{-x-4}{2}\right)^2 \\ & \Rightarrow(y+4)=-\frac{(x+4)^2}{4} \\ & \Rightarrow x^2=-4 y \\ & \Rightarrow \text { Focus }(-4,-5) \end{aligned}$ $\text { Also, } y=-5 \text { intersect }$ $\begin{aligned} & \therefore(-4)(-1)=(x+4)^2 \\ & 4=(x+4)^2 \\ & x+4= \pm 2 \\ & x=-2,-6 \\ & \Rightarrow d=4 \\ & a=\frac{1}{2}\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 0 & 1 \\ -2 & -5 & 1 \\ -6 & -5 & 1\end{array}\right| \\ & =\frac{1}{2}|[1(-5+5)+1(10-30)]| \\ & =\frac{1}{2}(20) \\ & a=10 \\ & \therefore \quad a+d=14\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let a line perpendicular to the line $2 x-y=10$ touch the parabola $y^2=4(x-9)$ at the point $P$. The distance of the point $P$ from the centre of the circle $x^2+y^2-14 x-8 y+56=0$ is __________
question: Let a line perpendicular to the line $2 x-y=10$ touch the parabola $y^2=4(x-9)$ at the point $P$. The distance of the point $P$ from the centre of the circle $x^2+y^2-14 x-8 y+56=0$ is __________
solution:
$y^2=4(x-9)$
slope of tangent $=\frac{-1}{2}$
Point of contact $\mathrm{P}\left(9+\frac{1}{\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)^2}, \frac{2 \times 1}{\frac{-1}{2}}\right)$
$\begin{aligned} & P(13,-4) \\ & \text { center of circle } C(7,4) \\ & \text { distance } C P=\sqrt{(13-7)^2+(-4-4)^2} \\ & =10\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the parabola $y = x^2 + px + q$ passing through the point $(1, -1)$ be such that the distance between its vertex and the $x$-axis is minimum. Then the value of $p^2 + q^2$ is:
question: Let the parabola $y = x^2 + px + q$ passing through the point $(1, -1)$ be such that the distance between its vertex and the $x$-axis is minimum. Then the value of $p^2 + q^2$ is:
solution:
Given the equation of the parabola $y = x^2 + px + q$.

Since it passes through $(1, -1)$, we have:

$-1 = 1 + p + q \Rightarrow q = -p - 2$

The vertex of the parabola $y = x^2 + px + q$ occurs at $x = -\dfrac{p}{2}$.

The $y$-coordinate of the vertex is:

$y_v = \left(-\dfrac{p}{2}\right)^2 + p\left(-\dfrac{p}{2}\right) + q = -\dfrac{p^2}{4} + q$

The distance between the vertex and the $x$-axis is $|y_v|$. Substituting $q = -p - 2$ into the expression for $y_v$:

$|y_v| = \left|-\dfrac{p^2}{4} - p - 2\right| = \dfrac{1}{4}|p^2 + 4p + 8| = \dfrac{1}{4}|(p+2)^2 + 4|$

To minimize this distance, the term $(p+2)^2$ must be minimum. Since $(p+2)^2 \ge 0$ for all real $p$, the minimum value occurs at $p = -2$.

Substituting $p = -2$ into $q = -p - 2$, we get:

$q = -(-2) - 2 = 0$

Therefore, the value of $p^2 + q^2$ is:

$p^2 + q^2 = (-2)^2 + 0^2 = 4$

Answer: $4$

subject: Mathematics
q: The radius of the smallest circle which touches the parabolas $y=x^2+2$ and $x=y^2+2$ is
question: The radius of the smallest circle which touches the parabolas $y=x^2+2$ and $x=y^2+2$ is
solution:
The given parabolas are symmetric about the line $y=x$

Tangents at $\mathrm{A} \& \mathrm{~B}$ must be parallel to $\mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x}$ line, so slope of the tangents $=1$
$\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{\min A}=1=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{\min B}$
For point $\mathrm{B}, \mathrm{y}=\mathrm{x}^2+2$
$\begin{gathered}
\frac{d y}{d x}=2 x=1 \\ x=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow y=\frac{9}{4} \\ \therefore \text { Point } B=\left(\frac{1}{2}, \frac{9}{4}\right) \Rightarrow \text { Point } \mathrm{A}=\left(\frac{9}{4}, \frac{1}{2}\right) \\ \mathrm{AB}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{9}{4}\right)^2+\left(\frac{9}{4}-\frac{1}{2}\right)^2} \\ =\sqrt{\frac{98}{16}}=\frac{7 \sqrt{2}}{4}
\end{gathered}$
Radius $==\frac{7 \sqrt{2}}{8}$

subject: Mathematics
q: The equations of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> of a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>λ</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>λ</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>λ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>λ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> respectively. Its vertex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math>axis and its orthocentre is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. The length of the tangent from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> to the part of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi></math> in the first quadrant is
question: The equations of sides <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>C</mi></math> of a triangle <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi><mi>C</mi></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>λ</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>λ</mi><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>λ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>λ</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> respectively. Its vertex <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> is on the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>-</mo></math>axis and its orthocentre is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>. The length of the tangent from the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> to the part of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi></math> in the first quadrant is
solution:
Given,

The equations of sides AB and AC of a triangle ABC and have orthocentre at 1,2

So, AB≡λ+1x+λy=4 ........1

And AC≡λx+1-λy+λ=0 ......2 

Also given vertex A is on the y-axis

So, putting the value x=0 in equation 1 & 2 we get,

y=4λ & y=λλ-1

⇒4λ=λλ-1

⇒λ=2

Now putting the value of λ in both equation we get,

AB≡3x+2y=4 ........3

AC≡2x-y+2=0 ......4

Now solving above equation we get, A0,2

Now let Cα,2α+2 {using equation 4}

Now we know that slopes of perpendicular lines is -1,

So, from diagram slope of altitude through C×slope of AB we get,

2αα-1×-32=-1

⇒α=-12

Hence, point C will be C-12,1

Now let the equation of tangent be y=mx+32m which passes through point C-12,1

So, 1=m-12+32m

⇒m2+2m-3=0

⇒m=1, -3

Also we know that point of contact of tangent to parabola is given by am2,2am≡32,3

Hence, CT=32+122+3-12=8=22

subject: Mathematics
q: If the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-intercept of a focal chord of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>, then the length of this chord is equal to _____ .
question: If the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-intercept of a focal chord of the parabola <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>4</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>, then the length of this chord is equal to _____ .
solution:
Given,

The x-intercept of a focal chord of the parabola y2=8x+4y+4 is 3,

Now simplifying equation of the given parabola we get,

y2=8x+4y+4

⇒y-22=8x+1

Now on comparing with standard parabola y2=4ax we get,

a=2, X=x+1, Y=y-2

Hence, the vertex is -1,2 and focus is 1,2

So, equation of line passing through focus is given by y-2=mx-1

Now, putting 3,0 {as given x-intercept of focal chord is 3} in the above line we get, m=-1 

So, equation of chord will be y=3-x,

Now putting the value of y=3-x in parabola y-22=8x+1 we get,

⇒1-x2=8x+1

⇒x2-10x-7=0

⇒x1-x2=102+4×7=128

⇒y1-y2=3-x1-3-x2=x2-x1=128

Length of focal chord x2-x12+y2-y12=256=16.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let a common tangent to the curves <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>16</mn></math> touch the curves at the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to ________.
question: Let a common tangent to the curves <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>16</mn></math> touch the curves at the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>. Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to ________.
solution:
Given,

Common tangent to the curves y2=4x and x-42+y2=16 touch the curves at the points P and Q

Let, the equation of tangent be, 

y=mx+am, where a=1 is focus and m is slope,

So, equation of tangent to parabola will be, y=mx+1m

Also line is tangent to circle,

So, using perpendicular distance formula from centre 4, 0to tangent which will be radius r=4, we get, 

4m+1mm2+1=4

⇒16m2+1m2+8=16m2+16

 ⇒m2=18

⇒m=±122

Now if m=122 then P=am2, 2am=8, 42

Now equation of tangent will be, x-22y+8=0

And Q will be foot of perpendicular from 4, 0 on tangentx-22y+8=0 ,

So, using foot of perpendicular formula  we get,

x-41=y-0-22=-4-0+812+222

⇒x,y=83, 823

Hence, by distance formula we get,

PQ2=1632+4232

⇒PQ2=256+329=32

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the length of the focal chord PQ of the parabola $y^2=12 x$ be 15 units. If the distance of $\mathrm{PQ}$ from the origin is $\mathrm{p}$, then $10 \mathrm{p}^2$ is equal to _______
question: Let the length of the focal chord PQ of the parabola $y^2=12 x$ be 15 units. If the distance of $\mathrm{PQ}$ from the origin is $\mathrm{p}$, then $10 \mathrm{p}^2$ is equal to _______
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { length of focal chord }=4 a \operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta=15 \\ & 12 \operatorname{cosec}^2 \theta=15 \\ & \sin ^2 \theta=\frac{4}{5} \\ & \tan ^2 \theta=4 \\ & \tan \theta=2 \\ & \text { equation } \frac{y-0}{x-3}=2 \\ & y=2 x-6 \\ & 2 x-y-6=0 \\ & P=\frac{6}{\sqrt{5}} \\ & 10 p^2=10 \cdot \frac{36}{5}=72\end{aligned}$

q: The normal at the point $\left(\mathrm{bt}_1{ }^2, 2 \mathrm{bt}_1\right)$ on a parabola meets the parabola again in the point $\left(\mathrm{bt}_2{ }^2, 2 \mathrm{bt}_2\right)$, then
question: The normal at the point $\left(\mathrm{bt}_1{ }^2, 2 \mathrm{bt}_1\right)$ on a parabola meets the parabola again in the point $\left(\mathrm{bt}_2{ }^2, 2 \mathrm{bt}_2\right)$, then
solution: Fundamental theorem (fact) $t_2=-t_1-\frac{2}{t_1}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If the area of the triangle whose one vertex is at the vertex of the parabola, $y^{2}+4\left(x-a^{2}\right)=0$ and the other two vertices are the points of intersection of the parabola and $y$ -axis, is 250 sq. units, then a value of 'a' is :
question: If the area of the triangle whose one vertex is at the vertex of the parabola, $y^{2}+4\left(x-a^{2}\right)=0$ and the other two vertices are the points of intersection of the parabola and $y$ -axis, is 250 sq. units, then a value of 'a' is :
solution:
$y^{t}=-4\left(x-a^{2}\right)$
$\mathrm{Area}=\frac{1}{2}(4 a)\left(a^{2}\right)=2 a^{3}$ Since $2 a^{3}=250 \Rightarrow a=5$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the shortest distance from $(\mathrm{a}, 0), \mathrm{a}\gt0$, to the parabola $y^2=4 x$ be 4 . Then the equation of the circle passing through the point $(a, 0)$ and the focus of the parabola, and having its centre on the axis of the parabola is :
question: Let the shortest distance from $(\mathrm{a}, 0), \mathrm{a}\gt0$, to the parabola $y^2=4 x$ be 4 . Then the equation of the circle passing through the point $(a, 0)$ and the focus of the parabola, and having its centre on the axis of the parabola is :
solution: Normal at P $\begin{aligned} & y+ t x=2 t+t^3 \\ & \uparrow \\ &(\mathrm{a}, 0) \\ & \mathrm{at}= 2 \mathrm{t}+\mathrm{t}^3 \\ & \mathrm{a}= 2+\mathrm{t}^2 \\ & \mathbb{R}\left(2+\mathrm{t}^2, 0\right) \end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{PR}=4 \Rightarrow 4+4 \mathrm{t}^2=16 \\ & 4 \mathrm{t}^2=12 \Rightarrow \mathrm{t}^2=3 \\ & \mathrm{a}=5 \mathbb{R}(5,0) \end{aligned}$ Focus $(1,0)$ $(1,0) \&(5,0)$ will be tha end pts. of diameter $\Rightarrow \mathrm{Eg}^{\mathrm{n}}$ of circle is $\begin{aligned} & (x-1)(x-5)+y^2=0 \\ & x^2+y^2-6 x+5=0 \end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></math> is the normal at a point on the parabola <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>x</mi></math> whose focal distance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> units, then <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:
question: If <math><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi></math> is the normal at a point on the parabola <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>x</mi></math> whose focal distance is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>8</mn></math> units, then <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:
solution:
Let the point on the parabola be P( a t 2 , 2at ) where a=2.

Given distance of this point from focus be 8 units

⇒  at2-a2+4a2t2=64

⇒      at2+1=8

⇒     t2+1=4 ⇒ t2=3

⇒    t=  ±3

Since y=mx+c is normal to the parabola

⇒c=2at+a t 3

⇒c=103

subject: Mathematics
q: Equation of a common tangent to the circle, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and the parabola, <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math> is:
question: Equation of a common tangent to the circle, <math><msup><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and the parabola, <math><msup><mrow><mi>y</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi></math> is:
solution:
Let tangent to parabola y2=4x is

y=mx+1m...i

If equation (i) is tangent to given circle whose centre is (3, 0) and radius is 3 then length of perpendicular from centre of circle to equation (i) is equal to radius of circle.

Hence, 3m+1mm2+1=3

⇒3m2+1=3m1+m2

⇒9m4+6m2+1=9m2+9m4

⇒m=±13

∴ common tangents are y=x3+3 or y=-x3-3

⇒3y=x+3 or 3y=-x-3

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math> be the focal chord of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>64</mn><mi>x</mi></math>, which is tangent to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. Then, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>(</mo><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to______
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>m</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math> be the focal chord of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>64</mn><mi>x</mi></math>, which is tangent to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn></math>. Then, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>(</mo><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mo>)</mo></math> is equal to______
solution:
We have,

y2=-64x

Coordinates of focus of parabola is : -16,0

y=mx+c is focal chord, hence

⇒c=16m  ...1

y=mx+c is tangent to (x+10)2+y2=4, therefore

y=mx+10±21+m2

⇒c=10 m±21+m2

⇒16 m=10 m±21+m2

⇒6m=21+m2,  m>0

⇒9 m2=1+m2

⇒m=122 & c=82

Then,

42m+c=421722=34

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the latus rectum of the parabola y2=4x be the common chord to the circles C1 and C2 each of them having radius 25. Then, the distance between the centres of the circles C1 and C2 is :
question: Let the latus rectum of the parabola y2=4x be the common chord to the circles C1 and C2 each of them having radius 25. Then, the distance between the centres of the circles C1 and C2 is :
solution:
Co-ordinates of focus of parabola will be S=a, 0=1, 0.

Now, length of latus-rectum =4.

So, length of semi latus-rectum =2.

Also, ∠LSC1=90°

Now, radius of circle LC1=25

Now, applying pythagoras theorem in △LSC1,

LC12=C1S2+LS2

⇒C1S2=LC12-LS2=252-22=16

⇒C1S=16=4

∴C1C2=2C1S=2×4=8.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math> be the vertex and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> be any point on the parabola, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>y</mi></math>. If the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> divides the line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> internally in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math>, then the locus of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math> be the vertex and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math> be any point on the parabola, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mi>y</mi></math>. If the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> divides the line segment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> internally in the ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>:</mo><mn>3</mn></math>, then the locus of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is
solution:
General point on x2=8y is Q4t,2t2

Let Ph,k divide OQ in ratio 1:3

h,k=14t+301+3,12t2+301+3

h,k=t,2t24

h=t & k=t22

k=h22

⇒  x2=2y  is the required locus.
